title: Chapter 22: Debra Wu's Second Move
content: The letter was different this time.

Chloe knew it was different the moment Maya picked it up from the mail slot, because Maya's face did a very specific thing — a tightening around the jaw, a slight flare of the nostrils, a micro-expression that in anyone else might have been disgust but in Maya meant I am about to go to war and I am annoyed that I have to.
"What is it?" Dave asked from the couch, where he was editing audio with headphones on one ear like a person who wanted to hear the world but only at 40% volume.
"Certified letter," Maya said. She held it up. Thick envelope. Official stationery. The kind of envelope that cost more to mail than most people spent on lunch.
"For Chloe?"
"For all of us. Addressed to the apartment. Law firm letterhead."
Dave took his headphone off the other ear. That was the Dave equivalent of standing up.
Maya opened it. Read it. Read it again. Her jaw did the thing again, but worse.
"Elif," Maya called. Not loud. The kind of not-loud that was somehow more alarming than screaming.
Elif emerged from her room with a ring light still clipped to her phone and the specific focused expression of someone who'd been filming a story. "What."
Maya handed her the letter.
The letter was from Pinnacle & Sato, Intellectual Property Counsel.

Not the legal department this time — not the standard-issue corporate lawyers who sent form-letter threats and bills for imaginary damages. IP counsel. The specialized ones. The ones who understood that the modern battlefield wasn't about money or property or even reputation. It was about ownership of ideas, of personas, of vibes.
Specifically, Chloe's vibes.
*Dear Ms. Reyes, et al.,*

*We write on behalf of Pinnacle-Whitaker Consulting Group regarding the intellectual property rights associated with the social media presence known as @capybaradaily and the associated persona, content, and revenue streams (collectively, "the Property").*

*Our client asserts that the Property constitutes intellectual property developed using skills, knowledge, and methodologies acquired during the employment of the individual formerly known as Chloe Kim with Pinnacle-Whitaker Consulting Group. Under the terms of Ms. Kim's employment agreement, specifically Section 14.3(b) regarding Work Product and Derived Intellectual Property, all creations, methodologies, and commercial properties developed during or arising from the employment relationship are the exclusive property of Pinnacle-Whitaker Consulting Group.*
*This includes, but is not limited to, the calming "vibe" methodology, audience engagement techniques, and brand persona that form the basis of the Property.*
*We demand immediate cessation of all content creation, revenue generation, and public representation associated with the Property, and propose a meeting to discuss the orderly transfer of—*
Maya stopped reading out loud at "transfer" because her voice had reached a pitch that was no longer compatible with indoor speaking.
"Their calming vibe," Dave said. He'd taken his headphones fully off now, which was the Dave equivalent of putting on armor.
"They're claiming they own her relaxation," Maya said. "Her *relaxation.*"

"That's the most American thing I've ever heard," Elif said. She was still holding the letter, reading the parts Maya had skipped, her lips moving slightly. "And I once saw someone unironically buy artisanal air."

"They're claiming she developed her calming methodology using skills from Pinnacle-Whitaker," Maya said. "Which — she worked in brand optimization. She literally optimized brands for a living. They're saying the capybara account is just... brand optimization for herself."
"Is it wrong?" Dave asked, and then immediately held up his hands at the look on Maya's face. "I don't mean morally wrong. I mean legally. Is it legally wrong? Because morally, obviously, this is —"

"It's also possibly legal," Maya said quietly. "Employment agreements are broad. Section 14.3(b) — I remember it from when I helped her review the contract, and I said it was too broad, and she said it was fine because she'd never make anything outside of work anyway, and—" Maya stopped. Pressed her fingers to her eyes. "She was so tired then. She couldn't imagine wanting to make anything."
The room was quiet.
Chloe was vibrating.
The bad buzz was back.

She felt it start in her haunches — the same trembling she'd felt in the park that day, the involuntary shaking that meant her body was processing something her mind couldn't. It moved up her spine, into her shoulders, behind her eyes. She paced. Back and forth, back and forth, the same four feet of floor she'd claimed as her anxiety runway since the first Debra Wu letter.

The roommates watched her pace. Nobody tried to stop her. They'd learned that trying to stop Chloe mid-vibration was like trying to stop a washing machine mid-cycle — technically possible but inadvisable and likely to result in damage.
But then Chloe did something different.

She stopped pacing. She turned toward the window — the big one, the one that overlooked the street, the one that Maya had covered with plants because she said it needed "softening" and Dave had said it "needed a curtain like a normal person." Through the philodendron and the pothos and the jade plant that was somehow still alive despite everyone's best efforts, Chloe could see the city.

Brooklyn. Flatbush Avenue in the distance, a vein of noise and movement. The bodega on the corner with the cat. The laundromat that was somehow always open. The fire escape three windows over where someone had hung string lights in November and never taken them down.

And beyond that — the park. The long meadow where thirty people had sat on blankets and breathed. Where Jordan Lee had said the account saved their life. Where Chloe had hummed a single note and felt the whole circle settle like snow on a still lake.
Something hardened in her chest.
Not the vibration. Not anxiety. Not the panicky, spiraling, what-if-they-take-everything-again feeling. Something else. Denser. More solid. A thing with edges.
Resolve.

She didn't know the word for it because she couldn't know words in the way she used to — couldn't label it in her mind with the clinical precision of her former vocabulary. But she felt it. A wall going up. Not the kind of wall that kept things out. The kind that held things in. The kind that said: this is mine. This calm. This hum. This ridiculous, improbable life I've built with these people in this apartment with this body. Mine.

You can have my email password. You can have my 401(k). You can have the four years of competence I gave you and the gray hair I earned in your conference rooms and the birthday lunches where you gave me a card that everyone signed with the same pen.
But you cannot have this.
She stood at the window and she did not vibrate.
Maya's phone rang at 4:47 PM.

She almost didn't answer — her cousin's number, and she'd already left two voicemails and an email with the letter attached, and she assumed this was going to be a "let me look into it" call that would ultimately become a "this is going to be expensive" call, and she wasn't ready for either.
But she answered. Because Maya answered things. It was one of her many annoying qualities, according to Elif, and her most essential one, according to Chloe.

"Hey, so," her cousin said. No preamble. Cousin Lena didn't do preamble. "I read the letter. Twice. Then I read the employment agreement. Then I read the letter again because I wanted to make sure I wasn't hallucinating."
"Are we hallucinating?"

"It's bullshit," Lena said. Not with relief. With the specific satisfaction of a lawyer who has found the other side's weakness and is already sharpening the knife. "The IP claim is based on Section 14.3(b), which covers 'Work Product and Derived Intellectual Property.' The key word is 'developed.' They have to prove she developed the capybara persona — or anything resembling it — during her employment or using company resources."
"She didn't. She was a capybara when she started posting."

"Right, and that's the part they can't prove, because — and I cannot stress this enough — their employee did not develop a capybara persona. A capybara did. The entity creating the content is, legally speaking, an animal. An animal cannot be bound by an employment agreement it never signed."
There was a pause.

"Now, they'll argue that Chloe Kim and the capybara are the same legal entity, which opens a different can of worms regarding, you know, whether a human transformed into a capybara retains their contractual obligations, which is — there's no case law for this, Maya. There's no case law for any of this. They're making it up and hoping you'll be too scared to call them on it."
"So we call them on it."

"I'll take the case pro bono," Lena said, and Maya could hear her leaning back in her chair, could picture the specific way Lena leaned when she was committed, shoulders back, chin up, ready. "I hate corporate bullying more than I hate my student loans, and I really, really hate my student loans."
"Lena—"

"Don't thank me yet. This is going to be a mess. They'll file motions. They'll drag it out. They'll make noise. But they don't have a claim. They have a letterhead and a theory and a lot of audacity."
"That's kind of their whole brand."
"Then let's rebrand them," Lena said. "Send me everything you have. I'll draft a response by Monday."
She hung up. Maya stared at her phone.
Dave raised an eyebrow. "Good news?"
"My cousin is taking the case. Pro bono. She says the claim is bullshit."
"Great," Elif said. "I love when things are bullshit but legally bullshit, which means they can still ruin your life for months and cost you—"
"She said she'd handle it," Maya said firmly. And then, quieter: "She said they don't have a claim."

The apartment settled around that. The idea that someone — someone with a law degree and a grudge against student loans — was on their side. That the letter wasn't a death sentence. That bullshit with a letterhead was still, at the end of the day, just bullshit.
Chloe had left the window. She was back on the couch, curled into her spot, her large body tucked into a shape that looked almost small. Almost.

Maya sat next to her. Put her hand on Chloe's back. Felt the stillness there — no vibration, no trembling, no buzzing. Just the slow rise and fall of breathing and the steady thump of a heart that defied medical science and the particular warmth of a capybara who had, for the first time since the transformation, decided to fight back.
Not with words. Not with a counter-letter or a legal strategy or a viral takedown thread.
With stillness. With resolve. With the simple, stubborn refusal to let the people who'd already taken so much take the one thing that was actually hers.
Her calm.
Her hum.
Her life.

The sunset came through the window, red and gold through the plants, and the apartment held four people and a capybara and a letter that was scary and a lawyer who was angry and a feeling that was newer than fear and stronger than it.
Chloe hummed. Low. Warm. The note that meant: I'm here. We're here. This is ours.
This time, she meant it as a promise.
